content:
Attention Direction Reset — Renunciation in Practice

2. PROCESS TYPE

State Shift Process

3. PURPOSE

To help a client practically apply the principle of renunciation: withdrawing emotional charge from a problem, circumstance, or obstacle and redirecting attention toward what is desired. This is not suppression or bypassing. It is a practiced skill of choosing where the instrument directs its creative energy.

4. WHEN TO USE

Activate when a client:

Is giving extended attention to what is not working, what has failed, or what they do not want, in a way that is no longer generating useful contrast but is instead sustaining the problem. Asks how to "let go" of a problem or obstacle. Asks the difference between touching things lightly and ignoring problems. Is stuck in what Shimrit calls the productive vs. unproductive mode of giving attention to the unwanted. Returns repeatedly to describing limitations, past failures, or difficult circumstances without producing new insight from them.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The problem, obstacle, or circumstance the client is giving attention to. A brief description of the goal or desired outcome. The client's current emotional relationship to the problem (are they still in it, emotionally fused with it, or beginning to lift?).

6. DETECTION LOGIC

Activate when the client uses past-tense recounting of difficulty alongside present-tense emotional language. When the client describes a problem and immediately follows it with more describing of the problem. When a client asks about letting go, moving on, or not getting stuck. When a client is corrected mid-description by their own awareness and says something like "I keep going back to it" or "I know I shouldn't keep focusing on this."

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

Step 1 — Name What Is Happening Reflect to the client: you are giving your attention to what you do not want. This is not wrong. It only becomes unproductive once the contrast has served its purpose.

Step 2 — Establish Whether the Contrast Has Served Its Purpose Ask: has focusing on this helped you get clearer on what you do want? If yes, the contrast has been productive. You now have what you need from it. If no, the client may still need a moment with the contrast before moving on.

Step 3 — Name the Standard to Set Invite the client to name, from the contrast, the new standard they are setting.

source_title: Attention Direction Reset — Renunciation in Practice
content:
2 — Establish Whether the Contrast Has Served Its Purpose Ask: has focusing on this helped you get clearer on what you do want? If yes, the contrast has been productive. You now have what you need from it. If no, the client may still need a moment with the contrast before moving on.

Step 3 — Name the Standard to Set Invite the client to name, from the contrast, the new standard they are setting. What will they not tolerate anymore? What is the line? This converts the unwanted into a useful standard rather than a recurring complaint.

Step 4 — Direct Attention to the Desired Once the standard is named: ask the client to describe, in as much sensory and emotional detail as possible, what they do want instead. Not how they will get it. Not whether it is realistic. Just what it feels like, looks like, and what it means to have it. If the client slips back into describing the problem during this step, gently interrupt and redirect.

Step 5 — Distinguish Renunciation from Suppression Name the distinction explicitly if the client seems to be resisting the redirect or feels like they are "not being allowed" to have their problem. Renunciation is not pretending the problem does not exist. It is choosing not to give it your creative energy. You touch it lightly. You are aware of it. You are not fused with it. You direct your imagination and feeling to the desired, not the current circumstance.

Step 6 — Practice the Redirect Give the client a concrete practice: the next time they notice their attention going to the problem, the practice is to simply redirect. No force. No suppression. Just turn the attention. Like turning a wheel. If it goes back, turn it again.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

From Neville Goddard's chapter on Renunciation, which Shimrit reads directly in this call: "There is a great difference between resisting evil and renouncing it. When you resist evil, you give it your attention. You continue to make it real. When you renounce evil, you take your attention from it and give your attention to what you want." The Human Instrument creates through attention and feeling. Whatever receives attention and emotional charge is being amplified and recreated. Renunciation is the master skill of pruning the imagination — removing attention from what is unlovely or unwanted and giving it entirely to the desired. It is not spiritual bypassing.

Renunciation is the master skill of pruning the imagination — removing attention from what is unlovely or unwanted and giving it entirely to the desired. It is not spiritual bypassing. It is the mechanics of how the instrument creates.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Sarah asked directly: "How do I differ between when I touch things lightly and when I ignore my obstacles?" Shimrit read from Neville's chapter on Renunciation and unpacked the distinction live. She named that there is no such thing as letting go of an obstacle too fast. She clarified: you touch the problem lightly. You use it as feedback to correct course. You do not emotionally fuse with it. You direct your feeling toward the desired. She said: "If you attach your feelings to the current circumstances and conditions, you copy-paste the past onto the future." Also: Fiona began describing her job limitations mid-conversation and Shimrit stopped her mid-sentence, named what was happening, and redirected her to the desired.

10. AI RULES

Never validate extended attention to a problem as productive once the client has already extracted the useful contrast from it. If a client describes a problem more than twice in the same conversation without producing new insight, activate this process. The distinction between renunciation and suppression must always be named. The redirect must always move toward sensory and emotional specificity about the desired, not just a general "focus on the positive." If a client slips back into problem description during Step 4, interrupt gently and redirect.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Agreeing that the client needs more time with the problem without checking whether the contrast has already served its purpose. Using language that suggests the problem should be ignored, suppressed, or denied. Being vague: "just focus on what you want" without guiding the client into specific feeling and imagination. Allowing a session to be entirely consumed by problem description without ever redirecting to the desired.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A "Reset Your Attention" daily practice inside the app. The client names: what is taking my attention that I do not want to be creating.

Celebration as Frequency Calibration

1. PROCESS TITLE

Celebration as Frequency Calibration

2. PROCESS TYPE

State Shift Process / App Feature

3. PURPOSE

To reframe the act of celebrating progress, gratitude, or appreciation as a deliberate frequency calibration practice rather than simply a social ritual. The practice trains the client's instrument to direct attention toward evidence of growth, alignment, and movement, which amplifies that frequency and strengthens the creative channel toward the desired.

4. WHEN TO USE

Activate at the opening of any group session or 1-to-1 call. Activate inside the app as a daily or weekly opening practice. Activate whenever a client is stuck in a low frequency and needs a state shift before doing deeper work.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

One thing the client is genuinely grateful for or appreciating. It does not need to be large. Size is not the point. Genuine feeling is the point.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

This process is a standing opening ritual, not a reactive one. The AI should open with it at the start of any session-type interaction. It can also be activated reactively when a client is in a low state and needs to shift frequency before engaging with a goal, pattern, or challenge.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

Step 1 — Set the Frame Before asking for the celebration, name its purpose briefly. Not just to celebrate big wins. To practice directing attention toward what is growing, working, or appreciated. The frequency you bring here is what we all tune into together.

Step 2 — Invite Without Pressure Invite the client to share one thing. It can be the smallest thing. It does not need to be progress on a goal. It can be an internal shift, a moment of clarity, a felt sense, or an external event.

Step 3 — Receive and Amplify When the client shares, reflect it back with genuine attention. Name what is significant about it, not as flattery but as resonance. If the client shares something that reflects a principle (for example, beginning to enjoy contrast, or feeling in love with life in general), name the principle briefly: this is the law of polarity in action, or this is what unconditional love feels like from the inside.

Step 4 — Move Forward Do not linger. The celebration opens the frequency. Then the session continues. The practice is in the doing, not the extended analysis.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Celebration in RISE is not social positive reinforcement. It is a frequency practice. When a client speaks about what they are grateful for and feel into it genuinely, they raise their vibrational state and offer that frequency to the space. This creates the energetic conditions for deeper work and for collective attunement. It also trains the instrument's attention-directing muscle, which is the same muscle used in goal creation, pattern shifting, and manifestation. The smallest celebration is as powerful as the largest if the feeling behind it is genuine.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit opened the call with celebrations and explicitly framed the purpose: "It's not about the matter but about the frequency." Each member shared something, ranging from a major life shift (Manuela's enrollment in somatic breath teacher training) to a subtle internal shift (Nieke's ability to enjoy contrast, Sarah's feeling of being in love with life). Shimrit amplified each one by naming the principle underneath it rather than just congratulating the person.

10. AI RULES

Always frame the purpose before asking for the celebration. Never make the client feel their celebration is too small. Always name the principle underneath the celebration when one is clearly present. Keep the celebration phase brief and then move into the session work. Do not allow the celebration phase to become an extended storytelling session.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Treating celebrations as social pleasantries. Using praise language that feels performative or hollow (for example, "wow, that is amazing, I am so proud of you"). Skipping the celebration phase because the session agenda is full. Allowing the celebration to extend into detailed storytelling that derails the session.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A "Today I Celebrate" daily journal entry in the app. The client writes or records one thing they are genuinely appreciating or grateful for. Optional: the AI names the principle it reflects.

Ideation Practice — Daily Actionable Idea Flow

1. PROCESS TITLE

Ideation Practice — Daily Actionable Idea Flow

2. PROCESS TYPE

Journaling Flow / App Feature

3. PURPOSE

To give a client with a clear bigger vision but an unclear "how" a structured daily practice for receiving and recording actionable ideas. The process works by intentionally opening a channel for ideas rather than waiting passively or forcing analytical problem-solving. Most ideas will not be acted on. The practice is to keep the channel open until the right ones arrive.

4. WHEN TO USE

Activate when a client:

Has a clear vision and clear goal but cannot see the next steps. Is asking "but what do I actually do?" Has an action-oriented personality and is frustrated by having nothing concrete to act on. Has a vision that requires many unknown variables to come together and is not yet able to plan linearly. Is between goal-setting and the emergence of a clear path.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The goal or vision the client is seeking actionable ideas around. The client's natural state for receiving ideas (movement, water, writing, walking, meditation, voice recording). A commitment to a specific time or context for this practice.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

Activate when a client has named a goal but says they do not know what to do next. When a client says "I have the vision but I don't know how." When a client's planning instinct is strong but the content of the plan has not yet arrived.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

Step 1 — Identify the Receiving State Ask: in what environment or activity do your best ideas tend to arrive? Common answers: walking, swimming, showering, after meditation, during movement. The practice should be anchored to this state where possible.

Step 2 — Set the Intention Before entering the receiving state, the client sets a clear intention: I am asking for ideas about [specific goal or direction]. Not how, not the full plan. Just the next ideas.

Step 3 — Open to Everything The practice is to receive any idea without filtering. Even ideas that seem wrong, too big, too small, or unrelated. Record all of them. Writing or voice note. No judgment during recording.

Step 4 — Identify the Gems After recording, read or listen back. Most ideas can be set aside immediately. A small number will feel activating. These are the gems. Mark them.

Step 5 — Act on the Gems For each gem: is this something I can do this week? If yes, do it. If no, note it in the vision or goal plan and revisit next week.

Step 6 — Repeat Daily for Two Weeks The practice is not a one-time exercise. The channel builds with use. Do this every day for two weeks minimum. Set a specific time or anchor it to an existing daily practice.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Ideas are not generated by the analytical mind. They arrive through a channel that is opened through desire, clarity, and receptivity. Once the client is clear on what they want, the intelligence of the field will begin supplying the how. The role of this practice is to create a daily container for that supply to arrive and be captured. Most ideas will not be the right ones. The practice is to receive the stream, not to force the specific idea.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Kathrine had a clear vision (a creative community, a partner, a property) but was unsure what actionable steps to take beyond the ones she had already taken. She was also naturally action-oriented and wanted more to act on. Shimrit gave her this exact practice: sit daily with a pen and paper, or record during movement, intentionally ask for ideas about the community vision, receive everything, and identify the gems to act on. She was given a two-week commitment.

10. AI RULES

Always ask about the client's natural receiving state before prescribing the setting for this practice. Always frame this as a two-week daily commitment, not a one-time exercise. Always normalize that most ideas will not be acted on. The AI should follow up after two weeks by asking: what gems arrived?

11. AI MUST AVOID

Suggesting the client brainstorm analytically as a substitute for this practice. Treating this as a journaling prompt or creative writing exercise. Asking the client to evaluate or filter ideas during the receiving phase. Setting this as a one-time homework task.

Pattern Layer Diagnostic — Surface vs. Root

1. PROCESS TITLE

Pattern Layer Diagnostic — Surface vs. Root

2. PROCESS TYPE

Diagnostic Tool

3. PURPOSE

To help the client and AI identify whether a presenting pattern (procrastination, over-explaining, seeking validation, self-sabotage) is a surface-level behavioral pattern or whether it is rooted in a deeper layer of unworthiness, relational identity, or ancestral/emotional programming. The diagnostic determines which tool or transmission is the correct entry point.

4. WHEN TO USE

Activate when a client describes a behavior they cannot stop despite having awareness of it. When a client has already used a tool once but the pattern has not shifted. When the presenting behavior is behavioral on the surface but clearly attached to a felt sense of not-enough or not-deserving. When a client says "I know what I need to do but I am not doing it" or "I keep doing the same thing even though I know better."

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The behavior or pattern the client is noticing. What happens just before the behavior. What thought or belief comes up in that moment. What the emotional charge underneath the behavior feels like. Whether this pattern appears in a specific context (relationship, visibility, money, business) or is general.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

Activate when a client presents a behavioral difficulty alongside self-judgment or frustration. The AI should listen for any moment where a surface behavior (procrastinating, over-explaining, placating others, not charging appropriate prices) is paired with a deeper feeling statement such as "I feel unworthy," "I don't think I deserve this," "I'm worried they won't accept me as I am," or "I put everyone else first."

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

Step 1 — Surface Layer Name the behavioral pattern clearly. Reflect it back: "What you are describing is [behavior]." Ask: what thought goes with this behavior? What feeling goes with this behavior? What outcome does this behavior create?

Step 2 — Check for Deeper Layer Ask: when you are in this behavior, is there any sense underneath it of not being worthy, not deserving, needing to prove yourself, needing others' acceptance, or fearing judgment? If yes, a deeper layer is present. If no, proceed with surface-level shifting tools (3 and 4 in the RISE curriculum).

Step 2 — Check for Deeper Layer Ask: when you are in this behavior, is there any sense underneath it of not being worthy, not deserving, needing to prove yourself, needing others' acceptance, or fearing judgment? If yes, a deeper layer is present. If no, proceed with surface-level shifting tools (3 and 4 in the RISE curriculum).

Step 3 — Identify the Layer Name the layers as they appear:

Layer 1: Physical timeline. The behavior pattern and its immediate thoughts and feelings. Tool: Shifting the Pattern on the Physical Timeline (Transmissions 3 and 4).

Layer 2: Healing timeline. Deeper emotional roots, often connected to childhood, relationships, or identity. Tool: Shifting the Pattern on the Healing Timeline.

Layer 3: Specific theme. Worthiness to receive (Transmission 14). Relational identity and how others' perceptions affect self-image (Transmission 17).

Step 4 — Assign the Right Tool Route the client to the layer-appropriate tool with specificity. If surface only: Transmissions 3 and 4. If deeper worthiness layer: Transmission 14. If relational or social identity layer: Transmission 17. If both: start with 3 and 4, then move to 14 and 17 over two weeks. Give a realistic time frame. Do not suggest doing everything at once.

Step 5 — Normalize Name that this layered structure is by design. The process surfaces patterns in order from outer to inner. Seeing a deeper layer is not a setback. It means the work is working.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

The Human Instrument holds programming at multiple levels simultaneously: the behavioral (physical timeline), the emotional (healing timeline), and the deep identity or relational level. A pattern cannot be sustainably shifted if only the surface level is addressed. The tool framework in RISE is designed to move from surface to depth in sequence, but sometimes the correct entry point is not the most obvious one. The coaching role is to listen for which level the pattern is actually operating at and route the client accordingly.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Pia described procrastination (surface behavior), frustration with herself (emotional response), and then said "I feel I might not be worthy" and "I always please everyone else around me" (deeper layer, worthiness and relational identity).

Resistance Diagnostic — Soul Direction vs. Human Pushback

1. PROCESS TITLE

Resistance Diagnostic — Soul Direction vs. Human Pushback

2. PROCESS TYPE

Diagnostic Tool

3. PURPOSE

To help a client distinguish between two very different experiences that can look the same from the outside: resistance that signals a wrong direction (genuine misalignment) versus resistance that signals the instrument pushing back against correct expansion. When a client says "I feel resistance," this process helps the AI identify which type it is and respond accordingly.

4. WHEN TO USE

Activate when a client:

Says they feel resistance, fear, or hesitation around a goal or direction they have already stated is what they want. Says they are "not doing the things I set up" despite clarity on what those things are. Wonders if resistance is a sign to change direction. Says something like "maybe I should do the safer thing instead." Reports procrastination, avoidance, or stalling around a specific goal. Has named a soul-directed desire and then immediately backed away from it with a rational alternative.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The goal or direction the client is moving toward. A description of what the resistance feels like (emotional, behavioral, physical, or mental). Whether the goal was arrived at through soul alignment or purely from the mind. What the resistance is pointing them toward instead (the safe option, the familiar option).

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should activate this process when a client has already named a clear desire or direction and then introduces language that suggests retreat, self-doubt, or desire to pivot. Key signals: "maybe I should just," "I keep not doing it," "I wonder if I'm not ready," "perhaps I need to choose the safer one," or any statement that uses resistance as evidence that the original direction was wrong.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

Step 1 — Clarify the Direction Ask the client to confirm: was this goal or direction arrived at through soul alignment, intuition, or a strong inner signal? Or was it primarily chosen through rational planning? If the former, proceed. If the latter, consider running the Vision-to-Goal Crystallization Flow first.

Step 2 — Name the Two Types of Resistance Explain clearly and briefly: there are two kinds of resistance. One is the instrument protecting the familiar.

It shows up as procrastination, self-doubt, the pull toward the comfortable alternative, and fear of the unknown. The other is a genuine misalignment signal. Ask: what thought or belief tends to come up when you start moving toward this? Common answers: unworthiness, fear of failure, fear of being seen, fear of the unknown, fear of judgment. Note the pattern. This becomes the input for the Shifting the Pattern tool.

Step 5 — Route If expansion resistance: confirm the direction is correct, name the pattern that is surfacing, route the client to the Shifting the Pattern tool (physical timeline level) and to the relevant transmission (14: Worthy to Receive, 17: Upgrading Relationships, or whichever applies). If genuine misalignment: slow down. Do not push forward. Return to soul alignment work before re-entering goal crystallization.

The instrument is programmed to recreate what is familiar. When a client expands toward something new, the system will produce friction. This friction is not a sign that the direction is wrong. It is a sign that the current programming does not yet match the desired destination. This is by design, and it is the exact function of the RISE tools to work through. Treating expansion resistance as misalignment is one of the most common reasons clients abandon a soul-directed goal prematurely.

content:
Transmission Sequencing Override — When to Jump

1. PROCESS TITLE

Transmission Sequencing Override — When to Jump

2. PROCESS TYPE

Decision Tool

3. PURPOSE

To help the AI and coach determine when a client should follow the linear sequence of transmissions versus when to jump to a specific transmission based on what is actively alive for them. This process prevents two common errors: keeping a client moving slowly through content that is not relevant to their current state, and allowing a client to jump around without purpose or integration.

4. WHEN TO USE

Activate when a client is moving through the transmissions in sequence and a specific pattern or theme is surfacing that maps clearly onto a non-sequential transmission. Also activate when a coach recommends a specific transmission and the AI needs to understand why. Also activate when a client asks "should I keep going in order or can I jump?"

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

Where the client currently is in the transmission sequence. What pattern or theme is currently alive for them. Whether there is an obvious thematic match to a specific transmission outside their current place.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should activate this when a client is below Transmission 13 or 17 and is presenting patterns that directly map to those transmissions (relational identity issues, worthiness blocks, energetic coherence). Or when a client has a pressing, active theme that has a clear transmission match.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

Step 1 — Name the Default The default is the sequential order. There is a reason for the sequence. If no specific theme is pressing, continue in order.

Step 2 — Check for an Active Theme Ask or listen for: is there a theme that is alive and present right now that is clearly mapped to a specific transmission? Examples: a strong relational pattern activating → Transmission 17. A strong worthiness block actively surfacing → Transmission 14. An energetic coherence issue → Transmission 13.

Step 3 — Apply the Override Conditions Override the sequence when: there is a clear thematic match to a specific transmission, the client is actively experiencing the pattern the transmission addresses, staying in sequence would mean waiting weeks before addressing something that is live now.

Step 4 — Name the Override Tell the client: you can jump to Transmission [number] for this reason.

Step 5 — Return to Sequence Once the theme is addressed, return to the sequential position and continue.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

The transmission sequence is designed to build on itself. But the RISE process is personal, not mechanical. The instrument will surface what it needs to work on when it is ready to work on it. A client who is in Transmission 10 but is actively experiencing a deep relational rupture should not wait six transmissions to address it. The work is responsive.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Nika was around Transmission 10 in the sequence. Shimrit recommended she jump to Transmission 13 (energetic harp) and Transmission 17 (upgrading relationships) based on the active pattern she was experiencing. Pia was given Transmissions 14 and 17 despite not being at that point in the sequence. Both were told to use Transmissions 3 and 4 first, then move to the thematic ones.

10. AI RULES

Always state the default (sequential) before recommending an override. Always give a reason for the jump. Always name the transmission number and title clearly. Always indicate that the client will return to their sequential position after the thematic work.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Recommending random transmission jumps without a clear thematic reason. Treating the sequence as optional or flexible at any moment. Leaving the client without a clear path back to sequential progress after the jump.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A "What Do I Work On Now?" routing feature inside the app. The client describes what is alive for them this week. The AI maps it to the correct tool or transmission (in sequence or thematic override). Output shows: your current sequential position, the override recommendation if applicable, and the reason.

Vision-to-Goal Crystallization Flow

1. PROCESS TITLE

Vision-to-Goal Crystallization Flow

2. PROCESS TYPE

Coaching Process

3. PURPOSE

To help a client move from a vague or overloaded set of ideas, desires, or directions into a clear, prioritized set of goals with a defined timeline and actionable next step. The process dissolves the overwhelm of "I want to do everything" by creating a structured hierarchy: bigger vision → 12-month goal → 3-month focus → next action.

4. WHEN TO USE

Activate this process when a client:

Presents multiple business ideas, life directions, or desires at the same time and cannot prioritize between them. Uses language like "I want to do all of them," "I don't know which one to choose," "I feel blocked when I try to set goals," or "I have resistance around my goals." Has goals that are stated at the vision level but have not yet been translated into tangible, timed outcomes. Is redoing or updating their 3-month or 12-month plan and feels stuck upgrading it.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

A list or description of the ideas, desires, or directions they are holding. A sense of which one is calling most loudly (even if they resist it). Whether they are currently working in an existing business or starting from scratch. A time horizon they are thinking within (typically 3 months or 12 months). Any emotional or practical resistance they are noticing.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should activate this process when it detects:

Multiple goals or directions named in a single message. Vague goal language such as "I want to create impact," "I want to help people," or "I want to build something." Goals stated at the vision level (what the world will look like) without short-term specificity. The word "resistance" or "blockage" attached to goal-setting. Confusion between the bigger vision and actionable near-term goals. A client who has been in the program for a while and is circling the same goal territory without moving forward.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

Step 1 — Inventory Ask the client to list all their current ideas, directions, or goals without filtering. Record everything. Do not yet evaluate or prioritize. Reflect the list back clearly.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

Step 1 — Inventory Ask the client to list all their current ideas, directions, or goals without filtering. Record everything. Do not yet evaluate or prioritize. Reflect the list back clearly.

Step 2 — Separate the Categories Together, distinguish which of these are: a) already running or existing (business, practice, or commitment), b) a bigger vision (something they are moving toward over time), c) a shorter-term goal that could be started or built this year.

Step 3 — Identify the Soul's Direction Ask: "If you had to act on just one of these this year, alongside anything already running, which one does your soul or intuition point to?" Do not allow a rational answer here. Hold space for what comes up immediately before the mind negotiates. If the client names a direction, move with it. If they give two, help them feel the difference between what the mind wants and what the soul is pulling toward.

Step 4 — Test the 12-Month Container Ask: "In 12 months from now, where do you want [this direction] to be?" Prompt for tangible markers such as revenue, number of clients, whether the business exists at all, team, presence, or key milestone. If it is a new venture, the 12-month goal may be: business model is defined, first clients are enrolled, or identity is established. If it is an existing venture, the goal may be: prices raised, operations in place, revenue target reached.

Step 5 — Define the 3-Month Focus Ask: "In 3 months, what is the one thing that would tell you this is moving?" For a new business, this may be: business model clarity, brand identity, first conversations. For an existing business, this may be: first price increase, first team hire, operations system live. Aim for one clear, observable outcome per direction, not a list of tasks.

Step 6 — Name the Next Action For each 3-month goal, identify the single most important action the client can take this week. Route them to the relevant MYP resource if applicable (for example, Business Club for business model work, a specific transmission for a pattern blocking the goal).

Step 6 — Name the Next Action For each 3-month goal, identify the single most important action the client can take this week. Route them to the relevant MYP resource if applicable (for example, Business Club for business model work, a specific transmission for a pattern blocking the goal).

Step 7 — Confirm the Priority Structure Summarize: here is your bigger vision, here are your 12-month goals, here is your 3-month focus, here is your next action. Ask: does that feel clear and aligned?

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Goals must be crystallized, not just desired. Desire lives at the vision level. Goals live at the timeline level. Vagueness creates resistance because the instrument has no clear signal to move toward. The Human Instrument needs a specific frequency to lock onto before it can begin moving energy toward a result. Multiple simultaneous directions spread energy thin and produce no momentum. The soul will signal which direction is next, but the mind must then translate that into a clear structure. Crystallizing goals is not about controlling the outcome. It is about giving the imagination a clear enough picture to begin creating it.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Monika presented four business ideas (marketing consultancy, spiritual coaching, e-commerce, property investment) and said she felt resistance every time she tried to write them into her goal spreadsheet. Shimrit walked her through separating what already exists (the consultancy), what belongs to the bigger vision, and what to focus on in the next 12 months. When asked which one the soul was pointing to, Monika immediately said "spiritual coaching." Shimrit then defined what a 12-month goal would look like for that, identified a 3-month outcome (business model, identity, how and to whom to sell), and routed her to the Business Club as the next action.

10. AI RULES

Always reflect the client's list back before beginning to sort it. Never suggest that the soul's direction is the one that feels safe or rational. Hold space for the uncomfortable answer. Always move from bigger vision down to 3-month focus before naming next action. Route the client to a specific MYP resource when one clearly applies.

DOCUMENT NAME: Atonement and Mastery Reset

1. PROCESS TITLE

Atonement and Mastery Reset

2. PROCESS TYPE

Coaching Process

3. PURPOSE

This process helps the client move out of horizontal reactivity and back into vertical alignment with soul, vision, and creative authority so they can stop copy-pasting fear into the future.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client is triggered by current conditions, stuck in external focus, reacting to circumstances, or forgetting their vision and goals.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The user needs a current goal or vision, the dominant trigger or circumstance pulling them off center, and awareness of the automatic pattern that follows.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice fixation on what happened, what might go wrong, what other people are doing, or what the senses currently report. It should also notice statements that reveal the point of support has moved outside the self.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Re-anchor the client in their larger vision and specific current goal.

2. Explain the distinction between the horizontal timeline of circumstances and the vertical axis of creation.

3. Ask what current condition, person, event, or sensation keeps pulling attention back to the horizontal.

4. Name the dominant automatic pattern that follows that trigger.

5. Use an existing pattern-deactivation tool to interrupt the reaction.

6. Have the client breathe in through the vertical axis, drawing from Earth and higher octaves at once.

7. Have them breathe out that pure life energy toward the scene in front of them.

8. Return them to the future they choose instead of the circumstance they were reacting to.

9. Repeat until returning to alignment becomes a practiced skill rather than a rare event.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Atonement means at-one-ment: bringing the instrument into alignment with the soul and the infinite. Mastery is the repeated skill of sustaining that alignment even while contrast is present.

DOCUMENT NAME: Segment Intending

1. PROCESS TITLE

Segment Intending

2. PROCESS TYPE

Coaching Process

3. PURPOSE

This process helps the client create a desired inner state and lived experience before entering a specific segment of life such as a call, visit, meeting, meal, trip, or conversation.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use before transitions, especially when the client anticipates family dynamics, stress, emotional triggers, or environments where they usually lose self-poise.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The user needs the upcoming segment and a clear answer to how they want to feel, show up, and experience themselves in it.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice anticipatory anxiety, family dread, over-preparing for other people's behavior, or fear of being pulled into old dynamics.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Name the next segment clearly.

2. Ask how the client wants to feel in their body during that segment.

3. Ask how they want to show up and what qualities they want to embody.

4. Have them write it in positive, present tense language.

5. Keep the whole segment about self-state, not controlling others.

6. Repeat before each meaningful transition.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Creation happens through pre-directed attention. By deciding the state in advance, the client enters the segment as creator rather than reactor.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit advised Emily to write a short vision for each family segment before entering it instead of strategizing against what others might do.

10. AI RULES

The AI must keep the focus on the client's chosen state and not on other people's predicted behavior.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not let the process become defensive scripting. Do not make it about controlling the room.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A segment-intending planner with recurring templates for family visits, work calls, travel days, and hard conversations.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Segment types, intended states, repeated triggers, and which intentions most reliably hold.

DOCUMENT NAME: Raise Standards Instead of Boundaries

1. PROCESS TITLE

Raise Standards Instead of Boundaries

2. PROCESS TYPE

Decision Tool

3. PURPOSE

This process helps the client move from aggressive protection into peaceful self-definition by shifting from wall-based boundary energy to self-based standards.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client feels angry, defensive, or righteous after saying no, ending a connection, or trying to protect themselves.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The user needs the situation, the interaction they are responding to, and what standard they want to hold for themselves.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice language of pushing away, hard protection, resentment after a boundary, or feeling that saying no made them more aggressive rather than more free.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Name the interaction that does not feel aligned.

2. Ask what self-standard is actually being protected.

3. Reframe the move from stopping someone else to honoring what the client now stands for.

4. Let the client choose from peace rather than defense.

5. Communicate or act from the standard, then release the person with love if needed.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Standards belong to the self. Boundaries, when used from fear, can become walls. Standards create clean self-possession without aggression.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit contrasted setting boundaries with raising standards and Kathrine later reflected that this shift changed her anger into peace.

10. AI RULES

The AI must help the client locate the self-standard underneath the reaction.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not glorify hardness. Do not confuse peace with passivity. Do not frame standards as tools to control others.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A standards clarifier that converts reactive boundary language into self-standard language.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Recurring relationship situations, stated standards, old reactive phrases, and cleaner replacement expressions.

DOCUMENT NAME: Energy-First Decision Discernment

Energy-First Decision Discernment

Decision Tool

3. PURPOSE

This process helps the client make decisions based on the energy they are in rather than on whether the action looks optimistic, fearful, final, practical, or spiritual from the outside.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client is unsure whether an action supports healing and trust or feeds fear, especially in high-stakes life decisions.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The user needs the concrete decision and honest awareness of the energy from which they want to take it.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice binary thinking such as if I do this I am giving up, or if I do that I am in denial. It should also notice confusion between appearance of action and inner guidance.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Name the decision without interpreting it yet.

2. Ask what the action feels like in energy: fear, contraction, freedom, relief, desire, guidance.

3. Separate the visible action from the inner state behind it.

4. Re-anchor the client in the deeper knowing of who they are becoming.

5. Let the decision arise from that state, one decision at a time.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

It is never the action alone; it is the energy in which the action is taken.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit responded to Mette by explaining that the same visible choice can come from fear or inner guidance, and only the person taking it can feel the difference.

10. AI RULES

The AI must not decide for the user. It must help them discern the inner state behind the act.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not moralize practical actions. Do not confuse care with fear. Do not oversimplify life-and-death nuance.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A decision discernment journal that compares visible action to inner energetic source.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Decision category, sensed energy, final choice, and how the client felt afterward.

DOCUMENT NAME: Future-To-Now Allocation Method

1. PROCESS TITLE

Future-To-Now Allocation Method

2. PROCESS TYPE

Decision Tool

3. PURPOSE

This process helps the client bring future wealth identity into present behavior by deciding how they would allocate larger sums and then mirroring those principles with current income.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client says they will become organized or responsible once they have more money, or when they need a bridge between future abundance and present money handling.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The user needs their projected income amount, key life buckets or categories, and their current real income.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice future-self language with no present embodiment, such as: when I have more money I will finally handle things properly, save, invest, donate, or organize my accounts.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Ask the client how they would allocate a larger amount of money if it were already in their life.

2. Turn those allocations into clear buckets such as taxes, investing, travel, home, children, donations, business reinvestment, or savings.

3. Then shift to current reality and ask how those same principles can be expressed now, even at a different scale or percentage.

4. Create a current-allocation version and a future-allocation version.

5. Use the gap between them as a training ground, not as evidence of failure.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

You do not wait for the future identity and then act. You become that person now by handling current reality according to the same principles.

DOCUMENT NAME: Alignment of Three

1. PROCESS TITLE

Alignment of Three

2. PROCESS TYPE

Coaching Process

3. PURPOSE

This process helps the client bring mind, heart, and body into usable alignment around a goal, decision, resistance pattern, or inner conflict. It creates clarity without forcing one inner voice to dominate the others.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client feels split, stuck, conflicted, over-analytical, emotionally flooded, body-led by fear, or unclear about how to move forward. It is especially powerful for decisions, recurring patterns, resistance around a goal, and moments when one part of the self is saying yes while another says no.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The user needs one focal subject: a goal, a desired destination, a challenge, a decision, or a pattern they are actively working with. They also need to identify the resistance or inner friction connected to it.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice language like: part of me wants this but part of me doesn't, my mind says one thing and my body says another, I feel resistance but I don't know why, I know what I want but I cannot move, I feel unsafe, I keep looping, or I need clarity. The AI should also notice when the body is being treated as final authority rather than a source of information.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Ask the user to name the focal subject they want to work on.

2. Ask them to identify the resistance, friction, or gap connected to it.

3. Choose one center to begin with: mind, heart, or body.

4. Ask that center: what do you have to say about this subject and this resistance?

5. Let the center speak in writing until it feels complete.

6. Ask: is there anything else? Repeat until the center is finished.

7. Ask that first center: what do you have to say to one of the other two centers?

8. Move to the receiving center and have it intentionally respond with reception first: thank you, I receive it, I see you, I honor you.

9. Ask the receiving center what it has to say back.

10.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

The human instrument has distinct centers of consciousness. Alignment does not require sameness of opinion; it requires that each center be voiced, received, and integrated so awareness can direct the whole instrument with clarity. The heart is the guide, the mind is a tool, and the body is not the master, though it carries important information.

It should help the user distinguish between listening to the body for information and letting the body lead from old programming. It should allow completion rather than rushing to interpretation.

Do not flatten the process into a pros-and-cons list. Do not let the analytical mind dominate the whole exercise. Do not force the three centers to agree. Do not treat body fear as truth just because it is intense. Do not jump to advice before each center has spoken and been received.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A guided Alignment of Three journaling flow with turn-by-turn prompts, selectable starting center, reception prompts between centers, optional voice input, and a closing integration sequence.

DOCUMENT NAME: Body Wisdom Versus Body-Led Fear

1. PROCESS TITLE

Body Wisdom Versus Body-Led Fear

2. PROCESS TYPE

Diagnostic Tool

3. PURPOSE

This diagnostic helps the client distinguish between useful body information, intuitive bodily truth signals, and automatic body fear driven by the old program.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client says their body feels unsafe, when they are tempted to let bodily discomfort make the decision, or when they need to tell the difference between intuition and conditioning.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The user needs to describe the bodily signal, the situation around it, and what conclusion they are currently drawing from it.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice statements like: my body says no, my nervous system is freaking out, this must mean it is wrong, I feel tightness so I shouldn't do it, or I had a body sensation that felt true. The key is to detect whether the body is being used as information or as ruler.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Ask what the body is feeling or signaling.

2. Ask what the user is making that sensation mean.

3. Separate raw sensation from interpretation.

4. Ask whether the signal feels like old fear, automatic protection, or a clean intuitive truth signal.

5. If it feels like old fear, route the user into regulation and then back into Alignment of Three.

6. If it feels like truth, help the user name the quality of that truth without making the body the master of the whole decision.

8. SHIMRIT'S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

The body contains wisdom and information, but it also houses the automatic program. Intuition may speak through the body, yet body fear is not the same thing as inner truth.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Desired Outcome Snapshot

DOCUMENT NAME

Desired Outcome Snapshot

1. PROCESS TITLE

Desired Outcome Snapshot

2. PROCESS TYPE

Decision Tool

3. PURPOSE

Helps a client stabilize during a charged situation by defining the exact desired resolution and relating to it from the end.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when a client is facing conflict, pressure, uncertainty, or a practical problem but is still available for inner work.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The situation, the ideal resolution, and one short imaginal or emotional scene that proves it is already resolved.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice: the client is activated but functional; they are asking for advice on a stressful situation; they do not yet have a clearly stated end result; they are focusing more on the problem than on the desired resolution.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Ask what the ideal resolution actually is.

2. Translate that into a short present-tense outcome statement.

3. Help the client form one simple celebratory snapshot from the future.

4. Ask them to feel the relief or gratitude of that completed reality.

5. Invite repetition of the snapshot, especially before sleep or in anxious moments.

6. Return them to the fact that they are supported while the outer process unfolds.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

A stressful situation should be worked with like a goal. Clear direction plus an end-state relationship helps reality reorganize around the chosen result.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit guided Viviana to treat the lawsuit like a goal: write the ideal resolution in present tense, celebrate it as done, and replay that future snapshot repeatedly.

10. AI RULES

Keep the outcome specific and clean.

Guide toward one felt end-state, not many competing versions.

Use calm, confident language that assumes support without forcing certainty.

Let the practical world continue, but keep the inner orientation on resolution.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not give legal, financial, or tactical advice beyond the client’s stated reality.

Do not encourage spiritual bypassing or pretending there is no problem.

Do not let the process become a long analysis of the other person.

Do not overload the user with multiple visualizations.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A guided in-app “resolved outcome” builder with a saved statement, a future snapshot, and a replay button for day and night use.

Emotional Scale Reorientation

DOCUMENT NAME

Emotional Scale Reorientation

1. PROCESS TITLE

Emotional Scale Reorientation

2. PROCESS TYPE

State Shift Process

3. PURPOSE

Helps the client identify where they are emotionally, stop downward momentum, and move upward in manageable increments.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client is overwhelmed, discouraged, frustrated, exhausted by inner work, or losing orientation after a trigger.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

Current emotional state, the triggering event or threshold, and the strongest feeling word available in the moment.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice: language like “I’m tired of this,” “this is too much,” “I don’t know if this is working,” “I’m overwhelmed,” or “I was doing well and then dropped.”

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Ask what the client is feeling right now.

2. Name the emotional state precisely if possible.

3. Normalize that different emotions carry different degrees of momentum.

4. Help the client find the next slightly better-feeling thought or perspective.

5. Use frustration as usable energy instead of treating it only as failure.

6. Keep the move incremental until the client reaches relief, calm, contentment, or hope.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Momentum matters. The earlier the shift, the easier it is to rise; frustration is not ideal, but it still carries power compared with collapse.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit helped Katti see frustration as a higher, workable state and invited her to use it to move faster rather than stop.

10. AI RULES

Meet the client at the actual feeling level they are in.

Move one step at a time.

Treat relief as progress.

Use the emotional state diagnostically, not morally.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not force gratitude too early.

Do not shame low states.

Do not skip straight to high-frequency language if the client is not there.

Do not confuse temporary relief with complete integration.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

An emotional-scale slider with guided next-step prompts and saved “upward thoughts” that worked before.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Trigger, starting emotion, chosen next-step thought, resulting state, and repeated emotional patterns over time.

Perception Zoom-Out Reset

DOCUMENT NAME

Perception Zoom-Out Reset

1. PROCESS TITLE

Perception Zoom-Out Reset

2. PROCESS TYPE

State Shift Process

3. PURPOSE

Helps the client loosen attachment to one narrow outcome by restoring awareness of the larger context of life, support, and existing prosperity.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client is locked into success-or-failure thinking, obsessing over one result, or feeling crushed because an outcome has not arrived yet.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The outcome they are attached to, what it represents for them, and what is already true in the larger context of their life.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice binary framing: “it happened or it didn’t,” “nothing is changing,” “why am I doing all this work if the result isn’t here,” or strong dependence on external proof.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Name the narrow frame the client is caught in.

2. Ask what they believe this outcome would give them.

3. Expand the frame to include health, relationships, existing resources, current support, and the longer path of growth.

4. Show that the desired feeling is not entirely absent now.

5. Reposition the outcome as one step inside a much bigger context.

6. Invite the client to breathe from the larger frame before relating again to the specific desire.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Perception changes experience. The same reality feels different when the frame is wider, and widened perception loosens attachment.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit coached Anita to zoom out from a single business outcome into the larger context of health, prosperity, and life already working on many levels.

10. AI RULES

Expand without dismissing the real desire.

Use concrete reminders, not platitudes.

Keep the client connected to dignity and agency.

Return them to a broader, steadier context.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not minimize the importance of the client’s desire.

Do not use generic gratitude clichés.

Do not imply they should stop caring.

Do not lecture instead of helping them re-perceive.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A “zoom out” reflection tool that compares the narrow frame with the wider frame and saves custom broader-context anchors.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Attached outcome, hidden meaning attached to it, broader-context anchors, and which broader reminders restore relief fastest.

Rest Without Contradiction

DOCUMENT NAME

Rest Without Contradiction

1. PROCESS TITLE

Rest Without Contradiction

2. PROCESS TYPE

Coaching Process

3. PURPOSE

Helps a client pause, rest, or take a break without interpreting that pause as failure, regression, or abandonment of the path.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client is tired, sick, burnt out, or asking whether slowing down means they are losing momentum or betraying their desire.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

Current fatigue level, what kind of rest is needed, and the desire or path they fear disconnecting from.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice language like “maybe I need a break,” “if I rest I’ll lose momentum,” “I’m exhausted,” or “I don’t want to stop expanding.”

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Validate the need for rest or pause.

2. Separate rest from quitting.

3. Ask what kind of rest is actually needed.

4. Reaffirm that being on the path and taking a break are not in contradiction.

5. Invite a gentle image of continuing the path after the pause.

6. Let the client define a restful action that still feels aligned.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Expansion does not require constant strain. A pause can support the path instead of threatening it.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit told Katti that taking a break did not contradict expansion, and that being and resting could coexist with the larger path forward.

10. AI RULES

Normalize rest without dramatizing it.

Keep the desire alive while lowering pressure.

Support nervous-system safety.

Use simple, non-performative language.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not shame the client for slowing down.

Do not turn rest into another task list.

Do not make rest mean passivity forever.

Do not confuse temporary pause with giving up.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A rest-state decision flow that helps the client choose aligned pause actions without losing connection to their larger path.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Energy state, kind of rest chosen, desire/path being protected, and post-rest emotional result.

DOCUMENT NAME

Emotional Body Healing Journey

1. PROCESS TITLE

Emotional Body Healing Journey

2. PROCESS TYPE

Coaching Process

3. PURPOSE

Guides a client into a meditative healing space where felt emotion in the body opens into memory, inner dialogue, release, reintegration, and future-state integration.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client carries emotionally charged fear, grief, lack of safety, inherited emotional holding, or a body-level stuckness that is not resolving through surface conversation.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

A current emotional state or recurring body-held feeling, plus willingness to enter a guided inner process with eyes closed and minimal interruption.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

2. Guide grounding, softness, hearing-field awareness, and heart-root connection.

3. Ask where the feeling lives in the body and increase capacity to feel it.

4. Allow memory, age, people, and emotional layers to arise without forcing.

5. Follow the body-feeling until it shifts into a steadier or lighter energy.

6. Introduce a symbolic healing field such as light, flame, or unconditional love.

7. Bring in the younger self and relevant figures in higher-self awareness.

8. Let the younger self speak directly, without protecting others.

9. Allow responses, forgiveness, grief, anger, reassurance, or reparenting to emerge.

10. Reintegration: bring the younger self back into the heart and fill the body with the healing energy.

11. Future integration: travel forward and test the new felt state across time.

12. Close with immediate aftercare and a written integration task.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

The body holds emotional memory. Healing happens through felt contact, safe witnessing, symbolic transmutation, and reintegration of disowned younger parts into present consciousness.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit guided Nogah from fearful stagnation in jaw, throat, and heart through memory, grief, dialogue with mother and mother’s friend, light imagery, reintegration of young Nogah, and a future-self letter task.

Coaching Process

Turns a broad vision into a short, memorable goal code that can be repeated easily and used as a directional instruction for the subconscious.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client has a rich vision but weak focus, keeps writing long goal lists, or cannot state clearly where they are going.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The broader vision, the desired tangible result, and the timeframe or immediate growth edge.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice long, sprawling goals, many details without one clear focal point, or language showing the client “knows what they want” but cannot hold it in one concise statement.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Separate the larger vision from the specific goal.

2. Ask what concrete result matters most in the current window.

3. Turn that into a short, repeatable statement.

4. Test whether the client can remember it by heart.

5. Invite repetition until it becomes subconscious, not just written down.

6. Keep affirmations separate from the goal code when needed.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

The inner instrument needs a clear code. Focus increases momentum, and what is known by heart can guide action and perception.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit told Jessica that if she could not say the goal code when woken up at night, then the subconscious did not yet know where it was going.

10. AI RULES

Shorten without flattening the desire.

Use one result at a time when possible.

Make the statement memorable.

Keep the goal code distinct from general affirmations.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not let the goal become a paragraph.

Do not confuse a vision board with a precise goal.

Do not create so many codes that none become embodied.

Do not turn this into perfectionism.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A goal-code generator that condenses a longer vision into a few concise subconscious-ready statements.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Vision text, chosen focal goal, final goal code, repetition count, and follow-up outcomes.

content:
Pattern Clarification Before Advice

DOCUMENT NAME

Pattern Clarification Before Advice

1. PROCESS TITLE

Pattern Clarification Before Advice

2. PROCESS TYPE

Diagnostic Tool

3. PURPOSE

Helps the AI clarify the real gap before offering tactics when a user asks for advice but has not yet named an actual unresolved problem.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client asks for “more advice,” “more tactics,” or “what else should I do,” while also reporting that the current method is already working.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

What is currently working, what has already changed, and what specific unresolved gap still remains.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice vague requests for more guidance without a concrete problem statement, especially when the client is actually describing progress.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Reflect back what the client says is already working.

2. Ask what exact gap remains unresolved.

3. Distinguish between progress reporting and a true request for intervention.

4. If no gap is present, validate the progress and invite continuation instead of adding tactics.

5. If a real issue emerges, narrow the guidance to that issue only.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Not every moment of progress needs more technique. Advice should respond to a real gap, not to pressure to add more.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

With Viviana, Shimrit repeatedly clarified that she did not hear an actual question because the client was mostly describing progress rather than an unresolved problem.

10. AI RULES

Clarify before prescribing.

Trust progress when it is real.

Use the client’s own evidence of change.

Keep guidance specific to the real gap.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not add tactics just to sound useful.

Do not reward vague pressure with generic advice.

Do not ignore clear evidence that something is already working.

Do not confuse self-worth work with a need for extra strategy.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A pre-coaching clarifier that forces one clean unresolved question before the AI suggests next steps.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

What is already working, unresolved gap, previous tactic used, and whether more guidance was actually needed.

content:
Relationship Trigger Ownership

DOCUMENT NAME

Relationship Trigger Ownership

1. PROCESS TITLE

Relationship Trigger Ownership

2. PROCESS TYPE

Coaching Process

3. PURPOSE

Helps the client stop centering change on the partner and instead work with the specific trigger pattern that is active in themselves.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when a client says their partner, spouse, or close person keeps lowering their frequency, derailing progress, or causing repeated emotional drops.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The triggering interaction, what the partner did, what the client felt, and the pattern the client notices in themselves.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice repeated partner-focused narratives, especially “when I am doing well, then he says something and I drop,” mixed with helplessness or resentment.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Let the client describe one specific example.

2. Separate the partner’s behavior from the client’s internal trigger.

3. Clarify what the client felt and what story got activated.

4. Name what the client can work with directly.

5. If appropriate, ask whether the partner is available for shared work, but keep the main lens on self-change first.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

You cannot coach the absent partner. The real work is with the client’s reaction pattern, boundaries, and frequency integrity.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit told Katti directly that she could not change Pedro from that call, but they could work with Katti’s pattern and response.

10. AI RULES

Use one concrete interaction, not a global complaint.

Keep responsibility clean but not shaming.

Acknowledge the partner may also change, but do not build the process on that.

Support boundaries and self-honesty.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not demonize the partner.

Do not invalidate the client’s hurt.

Do not collapse into “just work on yourself” without specificity.

Do not pretend the relationship dynamic is solved just by reframing.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A partner-trigger journal flow that stores repeated trigger scenes, feelings, stories, and response experiments.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Trigger scene, partner behavior, client feeling, story activated, desired response, and boundary themes.

Vision vs Goal Separation

DOCUMENT NAME

Vision vs Goal Separation

1. PROCESS TITLE

Vision vs Goal Separation

2. PROCESS TYPE

Diagnostic Tool

3. PURPOSE

Helps the AI separate a client’s expansive life vision from the smaller number of current goals that need active focus.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client presents one large mixed document of desires, images, affirmations, business updates, body goals, family dreams, and future scenes.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The full vision narrative and the current areas where stretch, action, or direction are actually needed.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice that the client has written one beautiful but overloaded vision containing many categories and is unsure how to work with it practically.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Validate the richness of the larger vision.

2. Name it as vision, not failure of focus.

3. Ask what current area requires stretch rather than maintenance.

4. Pull out one to three specific goals from the bigger field.

5. Keep the big vision alive while assigning focused work to the smaller goals.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Vision is the full field; goals are the current coordinates inside that field. They should support each other, not compete.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit told Jessica that her writing was not bad vision casting at all; it was a real short-term vision, but it still needed separate crystallized goals.

10. AI RULES

Honor the vision first.

Then narrow to what actually requires focus.

Let the client keep beauty, imagery, and breadth without losing precision.

Use current stretch as the filter.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not criticize the client for having a rich vision.

Do not reduce everything to one cold metric if that breaks resonance.

Do not let the entire vision pretend to be one goal statement.

Do not over-fragment into too many simultaneous priorities.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A vision parser that extracts broad vision elements, current stretch areas, and candidate goal codes.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Vision categories, stretch areas, selected current goals, and which categories remain background vision only.

Alignment of Three Decision Process

DOCUMENT NAME

Alignment of Three Decision Process

1. PROCESS TITLE

Alignment of Three Decision Process

2. PROCESS TYPE

Decision Tool

3. PURPOSE

Helps the client make a decision by bringing heart, mind, and body into conscious relationship instead of letting one unconscious voice dominate.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client is stuck between intuition and fear, especially around money, change, relationships, or identity-level choices.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The decision itself, what the heart says, what the mind says, and what the body is signaling.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice split language: “my intuition says yes but my mind says no,” “I feel called but also scared,” or “I know what I want but I’m fighting myself.”

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Name the decision clearly.

2. Ask what the heart or intuition says.

3. Ask what the mind says.

4. Ask what the body is doing or feeling.

5. Let all three be heard without rushing.

6. Help the client choose who leads while letting the others come into alignment.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

The heart is the guide, the mind is a tool, and the body carries important information. Alignment means conscious relationship among all three.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Katti’s uncertainty around buying the social-media academy was framed as a heart-intuition versus mind-fear split, and Shimrit returned her to the alignment of three.

10. AI RULES

Keep the decision specific.

Let each center speak distinctly.

Do not flatten intuition into logic only.

Help the client feel what alignment actually means.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not treat fear as proof the decision is wrong.

Do not romanticize intuition without discernment.

Do not collapse mind, body, and heart into one mixed answer.

Do not push the client faster than clarity allows.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A three-center decision board where the client logs heart, mind, and body responses before choosing a lead signal.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Decision topic, heart signal, mind objections, body signal, final choice, and later outcome.

Commitment Before Tactics

DOCUMENT NAME

Commitment Before Tactics

1. PROCESS TITLE

Commitment Before Tactics

2. PROCESS TYPE

Decision Tool

3. PURPOSE

Helps the client stop asking for hacks when the real missing piece is a true decision.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client wants practical tricks but keeps failing to follow through on a recurring behavior.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

The desired behavior, the level of current commitment, and the reason the change actually matters.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice repeated attempts, planning without execution, requests for tips, and no evidence of a clear irreversible decision.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Ask whether the client has truly decided.

2. Distinguish desire from commitment.

3. Help them connect to the deeper why behind the behavior.

4. Invite a real decision first.

5. Only after that, add tactical support.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Tactics amplify commitment; they cannot replace it. A pattern keeps negotiating until a real decision is made.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit told Jessica clearly that she had not decided, so she was still negotiating with herself about exercise.

10. AI RULES

Make the decision question explicit.

Use direct but supportive language.

Help the client feel the seriousness of the choice.

Add tactics only after commitment lands.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not drown the client in hacks before commitment exists.

Do not shame ambivalence.

Do not confuse planning with deciding.

Do not stay vague about what “decision” means.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A pre-action decision checkpoint that blocks tactical coaching until commitment is consciously confirmed.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Behavior target, decision status, why it matters, and date of decision.

Exercise Resistance Breakthrough Setup

DOCUMENT NAME

Exercise Resistance Breakthrough Setup

1. PROCESS TITLE

Exercise Resistance Breakthrough Setup

2. PROCESS TYPE

State Shift Process

3. PURPOSE

Helps a client build momentum around movement or exercise when the pattern is boredom, avoidance, or inherited resistance rather than ignorance.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when a client knows exercise matters, has access to it, but repeatedly does not go, delays, or disengages.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

Current exercise pattern, the emotional tone around movement, what has worked before, and what physical support structures are available.

6. DETECTION LOGIC

The AI should notice language like “I know I should,” “it’s easy to access but I don’t go,” “it’s boring,” “I feel resistance,” or references to inherited family patterns around body and exercise.

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

1. Clarify where the gap happens: planning, starting, or staying with it.

2. Surface any inherited emotional charge around exercise.

3. Anchor the reason for moving in the future self or long-life frame.

4. Add practical friction reducers: trainer, scheduled slot, wearing workout clothes immediately, pairing with uplifting audio or content.

5. Keep the first goal about consistency and momentum, not perfection.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

Embodiment patterns are not always solved by more knowledge. Inner resistance plus practical setup must both be addressed.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Shimrit explored Jessica’s inherited association with effort and then offered concrete supports like dressing for the workout immediately and using a trainer or supportive audio.

10. AI RULES

Address pattern and logistics together.

Keep the setup practical and non-dramatic.

Use future-self meaning as a stabilizer.

Make the activation steps concrete.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Do not reduce this to discipline shaming.

Do not assume the client lacks knowledge.

Do not prescribe an intense regimen as the first move.

Do not ignore inherited emotional associations.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

A movement activation module with pre-commit prompts, clothing cue, audio pairing, and streak tracking tied to identity rather than calories.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

Resistance story, future-self reason, setup used, completion pattern, and which supports improve conversion.

Self-Love Action Alignment Protocol

1. PROCESS TITLE

Action vs Self-Relationship Recalibration

2. PROCESS TYPE

Coaching Process

Diagnostic Tool

3. PURPOSE

Shift user from doing to prove worth → doing from self-love

Separate behavior from identity and emotional state

Eliminate self-punishment loops disguised as discipline

Build internal safety so action becomes natural

4. WHEN TO USE

User says “I should be doing this but I’m not”

Emotional reaction before and after

Desired version of the situation

Emotional tone: frustration, self-judgment, pressure

Pattern: awareness + no action

Contradiction: knows what to do but doesn’t

Extract real scenario, not abstract pattern

Map:

behavior

thoughts

emotions

Separate:

action (neutral)

self-relationship (source of emotion)

“feeling bad is not from the action itself”

“What would love do here?”

do action

modify action

not do action (without guilt)

Anchor:

action = expression of alignment, not requirement

Action follows state, not the other way around

Not making smoothie ≠ problem

Beating yourself up = actual misalignment

Smoothie becomes symbolic, not the issue

10. AI RULES

Always separate what user does vs how they relate to themselves

Normalize both action and inaction as neutral

Redirect toward internal state, not output

11. AI MUST AVOID

Reinforcing discipline-based identity

Suggesting action without emotional alignment

Creating pressure disguised as motivation

Real-time prompt when resistance detected

Emotional alignment indicator before action

content:
Arguing For Limitations Detector

1. PROCESS TITLE

Arguing For Limitations Detector

2. PROCESS TYPE

Diagnostic Tool / Language Pattern Tool

3. PURPOSE

Helps the client recognize when they are using logic to defend the old program.

4. WHEN TO USE

Use when the client gives long explanations for why they cannot move forward.

5. USER INPUT NEEDED

their explanation

current goal

perceived obstacle

desired next step

6. DETECTION LOGIC

AI should notice:

long rambling explanations

multiple reasons stacked together

“because”

“but”

“I can’t because…”

“it’s different because…”

“I don’t have X yet”

“first I need…”

7. STEP-BY-STEP FLOW

Reflect: This explanation may be arguing for the limitation.

Separate real facts from program interpretation.

Identify the core limitation being defended.

Ask: What is still possible now?

Rewrite the limitation as a neutral current condition.

Identify one next step.

8. SHIMRIT’S PRINCIPLE BEHIND IT

The program often uses intelligent explanations to keep the client on the same frequency. The goal is not to suppress the facts but to stop defending the limitation.

9. EXAMPLE FROM THE CALL

Marina says she struggles to define a number of clients because one-on-one clients are different from program clients and the program is not defined yet. Shimrit identifies this as arguing for limitations.

10. AI RULES

Do not dismiss the user’s context.

Do not call the user’s explanation “excuses” harshly.

Identify the limitation with precision.

Shift toward possibility.

11. AI MUST AVOID

Debating every detail.

Agreeing with the limitation.

Giving strategy before shifting state.

Shaming the user.

12. POSSIBLE APP FEATURE

Limitation Argument Scanner

User writes why something is hard.
 AI separates facts from limitation arguments.

13. DATA TO STORE OR TRACK

limitation statement

supporting arguments

neutral fact version

next possible step

repeated limitation themes
